To maximize the average batting score \( S = 50 + 5n - 0.2n^2 \), we treat the equation as a downward-opening parabola. The maximum value occurs at the vertex, where \( n = -b / (2a) \). Here, \( a = -0.2 \) and \( b = 5 \). Calculating the vertex gives \( n = -5 / (2 \times -0.2) = 12.5 \). Since practice sessions must be whole numbers, we test \( n = 12 \) and \( n = 13 \). For \( n = 12 \), \( S = 50 + 60 - 28.8 = 81.2 \). For \( n = 13 \), \( S = 50 + 65 - 33.8 = 81.2 \). Both yield the same maximum score of 81.2. Thus, the optimal number of sessions is 12 or 13, resulting in a maximum average batting score of 81.2.

Next, we calculate the change in win probability \( P \) when sessions increase from 5 to 10. First, find \( S \) at \( n = 5 \): \( S_5 = 50 + 25 - 5 = 70 \). Then, find \( S \) at \( n = 10 \): \( S_{10} = 50 + 50 - 20 = 80 \). Now apply the logistic function \( P = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(0.1S - 3)}} \). For \( S = 70 \), the exponent is \( -(7 - 3) = -4 \), so \( P_5 = 1 / (1 + e^{-4}) \approx 0.9820 \). For \( S = 80 \), the exponent is \( -(8 - 3) = -5 \), so \( P_{10} = 1 / (1 + e^{-5}) \approx 0.9933 \). The change in probability is \( 0.9933 - 0.9820 = 0.0113 \). This represents an approximate 1.
